column2: A four-step response protocol for a fire in a healthcare setting: Rescue, Alarm, Contain, and Extinguish or Evacuate.
column4: At the first sign of smoke in the patient room, the nurse ran RACE in order, starting by moving the patient to the hallway.

column2: A four-step operating procedure for a portable fire extinguisher: Pull the pin, Aim at the base, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side-to-side.
column4: The aide used PASS on the small trash-can fire, aiming low and sweeping across the base of the flames.

column2: A wall-mounted manual alarm device that, when activated, sends a fire alarm signal to the building system.
column4: Pulling the pull station is part of the Alarm step in RACE and should be done even after calling the overhead code.

column2: A short phrase used over a facility intercom to alert staff to a specific emergency without alarming patients and visitors.
column4: Each emergency code is paired with a standard first response that staff are expected to know.

column2: The emergency code most commonly used to announce an active fire or a report of smoke in a healthcare facility.
column4: A Code Red overhead page is the cue to run RACE, even before you can see the fire yourself.

column2: The emergency code most commonly used to announce a cardiac or respiratory arrest requiring immediate resuscitation.
column4: When a Code Blue is called, the resuscitation team converges on the room while unit staff clear the hallway.

column2: A healthcare evacuation strategy that moves at-risk patients only as far as the nearest safe smoke compartment rather than out of the building.
column4: Because most hospital patients cannot walk out, defend-in-place is the default strategy under NFPA 101.

column2: A section of a healthcare building separated from adjacent sections by fire- and smoke-rated barriers and self-closing doors.
column4: Moving a bedbound patient one smoke compartment away usually puts them in a safer zone than attempting to leave the building.

column2: Moving patients sideways through the building to the next smoke compartment on the same floor rather than down stairs.
column4: Horizontal evacuation is attempted first because stairwells are slow and hard for non-ambulatory patients.

column2: Moving patients down stairwells to a lower floor or out of the building when horizontal options are not safe.
column4: Vertical evacuation is a last-resort step because it is slow and risky for bedbound patients.

column2: A manual valve on a medical-gas line that cuts oxygen flow to a room, zone, or floor.
column4: Staff should know where the oxygen shutoff valve is before a fire, not during one.

column2: A written record of an adverse event, near-miss, or safety concern used to drive system improvement rather than assign blame.
column4: Even a small trash-can fire that was put out by one staff member gets an incident report so the facility can look for patterns.
